items.q: Is relative atomic mass always a whole number?
items.correct: No — it is a weighted average over isotopes; chlorine's is 35.5 because of its roughly 3:1 mixture of Cl-35 and Cl-37.
items.misconception: Ar must be a whole number because you cannot have part of a proton or neutron.
items.random_wrong: Ar is always exactly double the atomic number.
items.why: Students apply whole-number thinking from mass numbers of single atoms to the averaged property of the whole element.

items.q: Does oxidation only mean gaining oxygen?
items.correct: No — the wider definition is loss of electrons, or an increase in oxidation number; many redox reactions involve no oxygen at all.
items.misconception: Oxidation always means oxygen has been added to the substance.
items.random_wrong: Oxidation means a substance has been dissolved in acid.
items.why: The word's etymology anchors students to oxygen; displacement and electrolysis reactions are redox without any oxygen transfer.

items.q: Why is calcium chloride CaCl₂ and not CaCl?
items.correct: Calcium forms Ca²⁺ and chloride is Cl⁻, so two chloride ions are needed to balance the 2+ charge.
items.misconception: Formulae simply pair one atom of each element, so calcium chloride should be CaCl.
items.random_wrong: It is CaCl₂ because chlorine atoms are heavier than calcium atoms.
items.why: Students memorise formulae as name-pairs instead of deriving them from ionic charges, so charge balance never enters their reasoning.

items.q: The electron configuration of the sodium ion, Na⁺, is:
items.why: It's 1s² 2s² 2p⁶. “1s² 2s² 2p⁶ 3s¹” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The oxidation number of sulfur in SO₄²⁻ is:
items.why: It's +6. “+4” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In the Brønsted–Lowry model, an acid is best described as:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: A proton donor. “A proton acceptor” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many moles are in 4.00 g of sodium hydroxide, NaOH (Mr = 40.0)?
items.why: Exact answer: 0.1 mol. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
